title: 'Plenty of AI isn't generative, and/or is not using someone else's training data' - Music Ally
text:
In September 2022, Drew Thurlow left his job as SVP of A&R at Sony Music Entertainment to focus on being a stay-at-home father, with a toddler and a new-born baby to keep him occupied. Six weeks later, ChatGPT launched.
“It was immediately clear to me that this technology would have a profound impact on music, at every level,” Thurlow tells Music Ally.
So began a path that led him to his current work as strategic advisor to music-tech investment fund Mindset Ventures; adjunct professor at Berklee College of Music; roving consultant in the music and entertainment industries; and author of the newly-published book ‘
Machine Music: How AI is Transforming Music’s Next Act
‘.
Thurlow explains that ChatGPT’s emergence reminded him of an earlier point in his career, in 2011, when he left his A&R job at Nonesuch / Warner Bros to be streaming service Pandora’s first music hire.
“Things really clicked for me when I got there. We forget about it now, but those early days of Pandora pioneered much of what we take for granted in music today: a music-centric tech platform that synthesised streaming music, subscriptions, advertising, brand partnerships, and dynamic digital licensing,” he says.
The way he tells it, ChatGPT’s launch spurred Thurlow’s desire to recapture the Pandora days in a new context.
“I had had a good run at Sony, but a tech-centric, boundary-pushing music company it was not. I knew AI would have to be adopted by the industry, I missed being at the forefront of emerging tech, and I was immediately curious about how that would start to unfold,” he says.
The new book sets out some of his opinions and lessons from the last three-and-a-half years, with a central thesis that is notably optimistic.
“AI is – and will continue to be – largely positive for artists, rights holders, and fans. That’s supported not only by a lot of the evidence we’re seeing now, but also by the long history of technological disruption in music,” claims Thurlow.
“The industry has been through these moments before. This one is different in some ways, of course, but the pattern is familiar.”
“When something new comes along, people understandably assume the worst. But just because something new enters the picture doesn’t mean it automatically replaces what we’ve built…”
Drew Thurlow
Other opinions are available. There are plenty of people within the music industry – and a lot of artists and songwriters – who are currently feeling much more pessimistic about AI. One of the reasons is fear of a replacement effect: AI music competing with human work.
Thurlow offers another take: that this is one of several “incorrect” public narratives around AI, music and human creativity.
“There are a few, but the one that particularly gets me is the idea that generative AI is replacing real artists. Not only is that not true, there’s a lot of evidence pointing in the other direction,” he suggests. “Artists and fans alike are experimenting with this amazing technology, but in ways that are additive to the industry.”
“I get it: music has been a tough business for the last generation. So when something new comes along, people understandably assume the worst. But just because something new enters the picture doesn’t mean it automatically replaces what we’ve built.”
However, Thurlow is happier about the music industry’s response to AI’s emergence, suggesting that “people at the highest levels of the business” are taking it seriously, in contrast to the reactions to Napster in the days of peak filesharing panic.
“File sharing was obviously a disaster, and the industry couldn’t have gotten that more wrong. Even in the early days of streaming, the major rights holders were slow to adopt it,” he says.
“By and large, those technophobes are gone. Of course, labels and publishers still have a lot to triangulate: what’s good for their business, how fans will react, and, most importantly, what their artists think about all this. So they may move more slowly, but I think that’s often born out of pragmatism and education.”
Thurlow claims that this can be seen in the licensing moves being made by major labels and independents alike, with AI but also other areas of music-tech.
“It seems like every couple of weeks a startup announces a deal with the majors and/or Merlin. I think that experimentation is good,” he says.
“I think rights holders understanding that they need to relinquish a bit of control over their IP and let fans help dictate how music will be engaged with is a healthy thing. The monetisation structures will flow from there.”
Thurlow also thinks that generative-AI technologies are hogging the headlines and attention at the moment, but points to the other tools and startups outside that sub-sector as also having strong potential.
“GenAI gets all the headlines but there’s plenty of AI that is non-controversial. Plenty of AI isn’t generative, and/or not using someone else’s training data,” he says. “A lot of this is back office or productivity tools, or platforms for fans to engage with audio at a deeper level.”
That’s reflected in some of the investments made by Mindset Ventures,
whose portfolio includes
Music·AI, Audoo, OwlDuet, Aiode, un:hurd Music, Alloy, BeatBread, Tone and Mogul.
“Much of the younger generation is not down with AI… they want nothing to do with it”
Drew Thurlow
One trend that Music Ally has been tracking lately are the growing number of studies suggesting that younger people – Gen-Z particularly – seem to be emerging as the generation that’s most sceptical on AI both around music and beyond.
See
this recent Gallup poll from the US
and
this music-focused study by Muse Group
for examples of that. Or, indeed,
recent reports of unrest among Berklee College students
about a course on GenAI music and songwriting being added to their curriculum,
taught by someone who is also an advisor to AI-music firm Suno
.
Thurlow is teaching at Berklee too, and he has noticed the scepticism too.
“I’ve been quite surprised at the student reaction. In short, much of the younger generation is not down with AI,” he says. “It’s an interesting dynamic because I’m 25 years older than them, but I’m all excited about this tech and I’m all up on it and they want nothing to do with it. Isn’t it supposed to be the reverse?”
He says he understands why, however.
“Beyond music, the narratives around AI are that tech bros are going to ruin the environment while simultaneously taking any hope you have of employment. And if you’ve come to college to perform, compose, or study an instrument, this tech feels existential to you,” he says.
“Our job as educators is to point out what’s real, what’s not, what’s hyperbole, and where historical parallels can inform this current age. When we can have that conversation with students, their attitude changes.”
Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
More by Stuart Dredge
